content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 17, 1991


Natasha Merkuloff
City Administrator
City of Calistoga
1232 Washington Street
Calistoga, CA  94515

Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
    Our File No. I-90-677

Dear Ms. Merkuloff:

    You have requested advice on behalf of Calistoga City Mayor,
Jim Hughes, concerning his duties under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1
Specifically, you have asked whether Mayor Hughes may participate
in decisions regarding the Resource Management System ordinance,
or portions thereof.2

QUESTION

    The Resource Management System ("RMS") is designed to address
the problems associated with limited water resources and limited
sewer capacity in the City of Calistoga.  A proposed revised
ordinance is now before the city council.

    Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit
Mayor Hughes from making, participating in making, or in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence decisions
concerning the RMS ordinance, or portions thereof?


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Because you have not asked for advice about a specific pending
decision, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor
with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-677
Page 2

CONCLUSION

Whether Mayor Hughes has a conflict of interest under the Act
which requires his disqualification will depend on the particular
city council decision and the decision's effect upon his interests
in real property and upon the sources of income to him.

FACTS

Mayor Hughes owns a real property residence located at 1004
Cedar Street, Calistoga, and a real property rental of $1,000 or
more located at 1004A Cedar Street, Calistoga. The tenant of
1004A Cedar Street, Polly Thacher, is a source of rental income to
Mayor Hughes.

Calistoga Realty Company also is a source of income to Mayor
Hughes by virtue of his wife's employment with that company.
Other sources of income to Mayor Hughes are lenders. These are
Napa Valley Bank and Surety Savings.

In 1989, the Calistoga City Council directed the Sewer and
Water Advisory Committee (SWAC) to prepare an update to the
Resource Management System ordinance. On July 18, 1990, the
proposed revised ordinance was reviewed by the Calistoga Planning
Commission. It is now before the Calistoga City Council. Staff
is recommending that the city council approve a negative
declaration and adopt the RMS ordinance with proposed amendments
to the appeal section.

One of the issues of the RMS is the allocation of water for
different uses, residential, commercial, and industrial. Another
issue of the RMS is that it gives preferences in terms of water
and sewer allocations to low to moderate housing projects.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. (Sections 87100 and 87103.)

The Act provides a five-part test to determine whether a
public official has a conflict of interest in a governmental
decision. First, is the official considering making, participat-
ing in making, or using his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision? (Section 87100.) Second, does the
official have any economic interests in the governmental decision?
(Section 87103.) Third, is it foreseeable that the decision will
affect the official's economic interests? (Id.) Fourth, is the
effect of the decision on the official's economic interests
material? (Id.) Fifth, is the effect of the decision on the
official's economic interests distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally? (Id.) Each of these elements must be met
before there is a conflict of interest.

File No. I-90-677
Page 3


1.  Making or Participating In A Governmental Decision

    An official makes a governmental decision when the official
votes, commits his or her agency to a course of action, enters
into a contract, or appoints someone. (Regulation 18700 copy
enclosed.) Participating in the making of a governmental decision
includes, among other things, advising or making recommendations
to the decision maker. (Regulation 18700(c)) An official
attempts to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. (Regulation 18700.1(a).) If Mayor Hughes has a conflict
of interest, he is prohibited from all these actions.

2.  Economic Interests

    The second issue is whether the official has any economic
interests in the governmental decision. Section 87103 provides
that an official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family³ or on:

         (b) Any real property in which the public
    official has a direct or indirect interest worth
    one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

         (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
    and other than loans by a commercial lending
    institution in the regular course of business on
    terms available to the public without regard to
    official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
    dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
    received by or promised to the public official
    within 12 months prior to the time when the
    decision is made.

                              ***

                     Section 87103(b)-(c).

    Mayor Hughes has several potentially disqualifying economic
interests: his real property interests (Section 87103(b)) and
sources of income to him of $250 or more within the prior 12
months (Section 87103(c)). Income includes outstanding loans and
his community property share of his wife's income. (Section
82030(a).) Income does not include his salary from the County of
Solano. (Section 82030(b)(2).)


3     An official's "immediate family" includes his or her spouse
and dependent children. (Section 82029.)

File No. I-90-677
Page 4


3.  Foreseeable Financial Effect On An Economic Interest

     The third issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect Mayor Hughes' economic interests.  The effect of a decision
is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood
that it will occur.  To be reasonably foreseeable, the effect of a
decision must be more than a mere possibility, however certainty
is not required.  (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development
Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70
Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 198, copy
enclosed.)  The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of
interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible
conflict of interest.  (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

     When a matter comes before Mayor Hughes and he suspects he
may have a conflict of interest, he should examine the matter to
determine whether any decision by him would have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on any of the economic interests
listed in Section 87103.  In Mayor Hughes' case, several economic
interests may be affected by the decisions concerning the RMS
ordinance.

     We note that the City of Calistoga is presently under a sewer
and water moratorium.  The RMS ordinance will make available city
water for new or expanded residential, commercial and industrial
uses.  This may mean that the real estate market in the City of
Calistoga will be affected.  It is reasonably foreseeable,
therefore, that a decision concerning the RMS ordinance may
increase or decrease the value of Calistoga Realty Company, his
wife's employer.

     Furthermore, we note that decisions concerning the allocation
of water and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase existing
residential property values.  (Weidman Advice Letter, No. A-90-
211, copy enclosed.)  Thus Mayor Hughes will need to apply the
following tests to each of his economic interests.

4.  Material Financial Effect

     The Commission has adopted a series of regulations to
determine whether a financial effect is material.  In order to
apply the regulations in a particular case, it is necessary to
look at the economic interest affected and then examine both the
direct and indirect effects of the decision on those interests.
If the effect of the particular decision is material under any of
the following criteria, the public official may have a conflict of
interest.

     A.  Material Financial Effect on Real Property

          1)  Direct Effect on Interests in Real Property.

     The effect of a decision will be material if the decision
directly involves real property in which the official has an
ownership interest of $1,000 or more.  Generally a decision will

File No. I-90-677
Page 5

directly involve the property if the decision concerns whether the
public official's property will be included in a zoning or
redevelopment area, a license for the use of the official's
property, or the taxes on the official's property. (Regulation
18702.1 copy enclosed; Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) provides a more
comprehensive list of when a public official's real property is
directly involved in a governmental decision.

2) Indirect Effect on Interests in Real Property.

If Mayor Hughes' property will not be directly affected by
the decision, he must determine whether his property may be
indirectly affected. Regulation 18702.3, (copy enclosed) sets
forth the standards for determining whether the indirect effect of
a decision will materially affect real property. That regulation
provides in part:

(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:

(1) The real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction
of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.

(3) The real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or

File No. I-90-677
Page 6


(B)  Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.

If the effect of the RMS ordinance decision on Mayor Hughes'
real property falls within either subparagraphs (1), (2), or (3)
above, the effect is material.

B.  Material Financial Effect on Sources of Income

His spouse's employer, Calistoga Realty Company, his tenant,
Polly Thacher, and several lenders are sources of income to Mayor
Hughes. He should remember that if any source of income of $250
or more in the 12 month preceding the decision is directly or
indirectly involved in the decision, he may have a conflict of
interest.

1)  Direct Effect on Sources of Income

The effect of a decision will be material if the decision
directly involves sources of income to the official of $250 or
more in the preceding 12 months. Generally a decision will
directly involve a source of income if the source of income
initiates the proceeding or is the subject of the proceeding.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

2)  Indirect Effect on Sources of Income

If the source of income to the official is a business entity,
Mayor Hughes would apply Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) to
determine whether an indirect effect of a decision is material.
For the purpose of this analysis we will assume that his wife's
employer, Calistoga Realty Company, is not listed on any stock
exchange or securities list, is not a Fortune 500 company, and
does not have net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000.4 Under
these assumptions Regulation 18702.2 provides, in part, that the
effect of a decision on Calistoga Realty Company is material if:

(1)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenue for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

(2)  The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

4
However, this assumption probably is not correct for the
lenders, Napa Valley Bank and Surety Savings, which presumably
have net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 or above. The
standards to be applied in order to determine whether any given
decision will have a material financial effect on these sources of
income are contained in other subdivisions ((a)-(f)) of Regulation
18702.2.

File No. I-90-677
Page 7


(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g).


If the source of income to the official is an individual,
Mayor Hughes would apply Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) to
determine whether an indirect effect of a decision is material.
That regulation provides that the effect of the decision on an
individual, such as Policy Thatcher, is material if:

(a) The decision will affect the individual's
income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or

(b) The decision will affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.

Regulation 18702.6.

Mayor Hughes should examine the likely financial effect of
any RMS ordinance decision on sources of income to him and
determine whether the effect meets the tests listed above.

If any of Mayor Hughes' economic interests would be
materially affected by any decision related to the revised RMS
ordinance, he would be required to disqualify himself from
participating in these decisions unless the "public generally"
exception applies.

5. "Public Generally" Exception

Even if the decision before Mayor Hughes meets all the above
elements, disqualification is required only if the effect upon his
economic interests is distinguishable from the effect upon the
public generally. (Section 87103.) The "public" is all the
persons residing, owning property, or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Logan, (1985) 9
FPPC Ops.1, 15, copy enclosed.) In the case of a city council,
this would be the entire city. If the decision does not affect
all the members of the public in the same manner, disqualification
may be required unless the effect of the decision is the same as
the effect on a significant segment of the public. (Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.) Therefore, for the "public generally"
exception to apply, any decision would have to affect a
significant segment of the City of Calistoga in substantially the
same manner as it would affect Mayor Hughes' economic interest.
(Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-
86-210, copies enclosed.)

File No. I-90-677
Page 8


Mayor Hughes' has two real property interests, his personal
residence and a rental. Decisions concerning the allocation of
water and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase property
values. The rule regarding public officials owning three or fewer
rental units is enunciated in In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC 62 (copy
enclosed). Ferraro provides that an official who owns three or
fewer residential units may participate in a decision affecting
his or her economic interests because the property interests of
such an official will not be affected by the decision in a manner
that is distinguishable from the effect upon a significant segment
of the public generally. Therefore, since Mayor Hughes owns only
one rental unit, he would not be required to disqualify himself
from participating in the RMS ordinance decision on account of his
real property interests.

     On the other hand, one of Mayor Hughes' economic interests is
a source of income to him, Calistoga Realty Company, which may be
financially affected and in a material manner by a decision
concerning the RMS ordinance. We do not have sufficient
information to reach a conclusion on this. However, assuming this
to be true, it does not appear likely that the "public generally"
exception will be applicable to Mayor Hughes because the financial
effect of RMS decisions on Calistoga Realty Company is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally or on a
significant segment of the public. Therefore, Mayor Hughes may be
required to disqualify himself from participating in RMS ordinance
decisions on account of this economic interest.

     I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Deanne Stone
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

does not have net tangible assets of at least $4,ooo,ooo.'l Under
these assumptions Regulation lA7O2.2 provides, in part, that the
effect of a decision on calistoga Real-ty company is rnaterial if:
(1) The decision will resul-t in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenue for a fiscal year
of S1o,000 or more; or
(2) The decision wiII result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional, expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or nore; or
4 Hor.r.r, this assumption probably is not correct for the
Ienders, Napa Valley Bank and surety Savings, which presunably
have net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 or above. The
standards to be applied in order to determine \.rhether any given
decision will have a material- financial effect on these sources of
incorne are contained in other subdivisions ((a)-(f)) 
of Regulation
La7 02 .2 .
(3) The decision will result in an increase
decrease in the val-ue of assets or liabilities
S10,0o0 or more.
Regulation ].87 02 .2 (q) .
If the source of income to the official 
is an individual,
Ivlayor Hughes would apply Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) to
deterrnine whether an indirect effect of a decision is naterial.
That regulation provides that the effect of the decision on an
individuat, such as Po1ly Thatcher, j,s naterial if:
(a) The decision will affect the individual's
incorne, investrnents, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities 
(other than real
property) by $r,ooo or more; or
(b) The decision wi1] affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under section 18702.3 or
Section La1 02.4.
Regulation la7 02 .6 .
Mayor Hughes should exarnine the Iikely financial effect of
any RMs ordinance decision on sources of income to hirn and
delerrnine whether the effect meets the tests listed above.
If any of Mayor Hughes' economic i.nterests $/ould be
materially affected by any decision related to the revised RMs
ordinance, he would be requj.red to disgualify himself frorn
participating in these decisions unless the ttpublic generallyrl
exception applies.
5. 
t'PubI ic GeneralIv" ExceDtion
Even if the decision before }{ayor Hughes meets all the above
elements, disqua 1i f ication j.s required only if the effect upon his
econornic interests is distinguishabLe from the effect upon the
pubtic generally. 
(Section 87103.) The publicrr is all- the
persons residing, owning property, or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9
Feec ops. L, 15, copy enctosed.) rn the case of a city council,,
this wouLd be the entire city. 
If the decision does not affect
atl the members of the public in the same manner, disqualification
may be required unless the effect of the decision is the same as
the effect on a significant seguent of the public. 
(Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.) Therefore, for the "public generallyrl
exception to app1y, any decision would have to affect a
significant segment of the city of Calistoga in substantially the
sane manner as it would affect Mayor Hughes' economic interest.
(Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-
86-210 , copies encl,osed. )
ｒ
ｆ
Ｏ
　
Ｏ
I"layor Hughes' has tr.ro real property interests, his personal
residence and a rental. 
Decisions concerning the allocatj-on of
i'/ater and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase property
values. The rule regarding public officials 
owning three or fet'rer
rental units is enunciated in In re Ferraro (]-978) 4 FPPC 62 (copy
enctosed) . Ferraro provides that an official 
who owns three or
fewer residential units may participate in a decision affecting
his or her economic interests because the property interests of
such an official 
wil-I not be affected by the decision in a manner
that is distinguishabLe from the effect upon a significant segment
of the public AeneralLy. Therefore, since Mayor Huqhes owns only
one rentaL unit, he would not be required to disqualify hinself
from participati,ng in the RMS ordinance decision on account of his
real property interests.
on the other hand, one of Mayor Hughes' economic interests is
a source of income to hirn, Calistoga ReaLty Company, which may be
financialty affected and in a rnaterial, manner by a decision
concerning the RMs ordinance. We do not have sufficient
inforrnation to reach a conclusion on this. 
However, assuming thi,s
to be true, it does not appear likely that the ttpublic generallyrl
exception wiLl- be applicable to Mayor Hughes because the financial
effect of RMS decisions on Calistoga Realty Company is
distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerally or on a
significant segment of the public. 
Therefore, Mayor Hughes may be
required to disqualify hinself frorn participating in RMs ordinance
decisions on account of this economic interest.
I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, pfease do not hesitate to contact me at (91-5) 322-5901.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 17, 1991 Natasha Merkuloff City Administrator City of Calistoga 1232 Washington Street Calistoga, CA 94515 Re: Your Request for Informal Advice Our File No. I-90-677 Dear Ms. Merkuloff: You have requested advice on behalf of Calistoga City Mayor, Jim Hughes, concerning his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1 Specifically, you have asked whether Mayor Hughes may participate in decisions regarding the Resource Management System ordinance, or portions thereof.2 QUESTION The Resource Management System ("RMS") is designed to address the problems associated with limited water resources and limited sewer capacity in the City of Calistoga. A proposed revised ordinance is now before the city council. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit Mayor Hughes from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence decisions concerning the RMS ordinance, or portions thereof? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Because you have not asked for advice about a specific pending decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 File No. I-90-677 Page 2 CONCLUSION Whether Mayor Hughes has a conflict of interest under the Act which requires his disqualification will depend on the particular city council decision and the decision's effect upon his interests in real property and upon the sources of income to him. FACTS Mayor Hughes owns a real property residence located at 1004 Cedar Street, Calistoga, and a real property rental of $1,000 or more located at 1004A Cedar Street, Calistoga. The tenant of 1004A Cedar Street, Polly Thacher, is a source of rental income to Mayor Hughes. Calistoga Realty Company also is a source of income to Mayor Hughes by virtue of his wife's employment with that company. Other sources of income to Mayor Hughes are lenders. These are Napa Valley Bank and Surety Savings. In 1989, the Calistoga City Council directed the Sewer and Water Advisory Committee (SWAC) to prepare an update to the Resource Management System ordinance. On July 18, 1990, the proposed revised ordinance was reviewed by the Calistoga Planning Commission. It is now before the Calistoga City Council. Staff is recommending that the city council approve a negative declaration and adopt the RMS ordinance with proposed amendments to the appeal section. One of the issues of the RMS is the allocation of water for different uses, residential, commercial, and industrial. Another issue of the RMS is that it gives preferences in terms of water and sewer
